metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the `gate_dispatch.py` wiring: confirm the floor is invoked in the produce path before emit (e.g., an integration test or explicit AC verifying the call site exists and is exercised).
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` as a file requiring changes ('invoke the floor before emit') but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies that `gate_dispatch.py` is correctly wired to invoke the floor. This is uncovered work.
findings.location: Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py (invoke the floor before emit)'; Acceptance Criteria — no AC item mentions gate_dispatch.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'The `region_gated_floor` is invoked from `gate_dispatch.py` (or the equivalent produce-path call site) before the sidecar emit, verified by an integration or smoke test that a dropped finding does not appear in the emitted verdict.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC cluster (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/llm/code_review/test_region_gate.py -v` for the behavioral tests, a `grep` or `ls` for the new files, and a `grep 'code_review_novelty' src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py` for the contract registration) so done-ness is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands (pytest invocations, grep patterns, file-existence checks) are provided for any of the nine ACs; all verification is described qualitatively. An executor cannot confirm done-ness via a deterministic command without inferring test file paths and runner syntax.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all nine ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the system-prompt assembly mechanism for `score_code_novelty`'s `run_chunk`: e.g., whether it uses a minimal inline preamble, calls a code-review analogue of `_resolve_system` with the diff text, or builds the system prompt directly from `code_review_novelty.md` via `prompts.get_prompt` + `prompts.resolve_prompt` with a `{"diff": diff_text}` variable — and add this to the Scope or What section.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'score_code_novelty mirrors plan-review's _score_floor_novelty runner wiring' for building the sub-call, but _score_floor_novelty builds its system prompt via `passes._resolve_system(passes.PASS_NOVELTY, ctx.plan_text, vcfg)`, which renders a `{{plan}}` template variable with the plan text. Code review has no equivalent of `ctx.plan_text` or `_resolve_system`. The plan says the diff is the domain context 'NOT a {{plan}}/template var', but never specifies what mechanism assembles the system prompt string passed to `run_chunk` inside `score_code_novelty`. Without this, the implementer has no specified system-prompt assembly path for the novelty sub-call.
findings.location: What / 'score_code_novelty' + 'NEW `region_gated_floor`' sections
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section under 'score_code_novelty', add: 'The system prompt is built by calling `prompts.get_prompt("code-novelty", ...)` then `prompts.resolve_prompt(prompt, {"diff": diff_text})`, mirroring `_resolve_system` but substituting diff text for plan text. The `code_review_novelty.md` prompt uses a `{{diff}}` template variable (or the diff is injected as part of the instructions block instead of the system prompt — specify which).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that `score_code_novelty` MUST include token-budget chunking (via `verify_request_chunks` or an equivalent) when bypassing `score_novelty` — not just `reshape_novelties` + `decide.novelty` — since chunking is load-bearing for correctness under real advisory set sizes.
findings.finding: `score_code_novelty` is specified to bypass `score_novelty` (which includes token-budget chunking) to gain access to `matched_prior_id`, but the plan only says it 'mirrors' `_score_floor_novelty` wiring without explicitly specifying whether it re-implements the chunking from `verify_request_chunks`. If chunking is omitted, `score_code_novelty` will silently send the entire advisory set in one over-budget sub-call when advisory findings are numerous, causing the novelty sub-call to fail or produce garbage — and the fail-safe (empty map → novelty 0.0 → carryover → no drops) would then suppress all drops rather than correctly scoring them.
findings.location: What section: 'REUSE unchanged kernel primitives' / 'NOTE: it does NOT reuse the kernel score_novelty wrapper verbatim'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'score_code_novelty wraps the full chunked sub-call loop (mirroring score_novelty's verify_request_chunks + per-chunk run loop), but collects both the novelty float AND matched_prior_id from reshape_novelties in the same loop — the only divergence from score_novelty is the richer return type {index: (float, str)}; the chunking, fail-safe, and window budget are identical.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact system-prompt assembly mechanism for `score_code_novelty`: either define a new `PASS_CODE_NOVELTY` constant + an alternative to `_resolve_system` that does NOT render `{{plan}}`, or declare that `code_review_novelty.md` is used as a static system prompt (no template rendering) and show how the system string is obtained.
findings.finding: The `code_review_novelty.md` prompt file resolves to prompt id `code-review-novelty` via `_packaged_prompt_files()` (file stem `code_review_novelty` → `_→-` normalization → `code-review-novelty`), but the plan uses the prompt id `code-review-novelty` only implicitly. The plan never gives `score_code_novelty` a named constant (like `PASS_NOVELTY = 'plan-review-novelty'`) to use with the system-prompt assembly call, and the body says the wiring 'mirrors `_score_floor_novelty`' — which calls `_resolve_system(passes.PASS_NOVELTY, ctx.plan_text, vcfg)`. Since `_resolve_system` renders `{{plan}}` from the prompt template, and the plan explicitly states the new prompt does NOT use a `{{plan}}` template var (diff context goes into instructions, not the system prompt), calling `_resolve_system` with the code-novelty prompt id would either fail (if `{{plan}}` is absent from the template) or produce a wrong result. The system prompt assembly path for `score_code_novelty` is structurally unspecified.
findings.location: What section / 'NEW code-review novelty PROMPT' bullet + 'score_code_novelty' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `PASS_CODE_NOVELTY = 'code-review-novelty'` constant and describe a thin `_resolve_code_novelty_system(cfg)` that calls `get_prompt(PASS_CODE_NOVELTY)` and returns `prompt.text` directly (no `{{plan}}` rendering), paralleling how the diff context is handled entirely in `instructions`. This mirrors how the kernel `novelty_instructions` + `prior_findings_block` already put all content into the instructions side.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit return type signature for `score_code_novelty` (e.g. `dict[int, tuple[float, str]]` or `dict[int, dict]` with named keys) and show a matching extraction snippet in the `region_gated_floor` description so both sides of the protocol are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `score_code_novelty` function's return type is never specified. The plan says it 'surfaces BOTH the novelty float (for the drop) AND the `matched_prior_id` (for carryover-labeling)' and that the `region_gated_floor` caller consumes both, but the exact return shape — e.g. `dict[int, tuple[float, str]]`, `dict[int, dict[str, Any]]` with keys `novelty`/`matched_prior_id`, or some other structure — is left undefined. Without this, the `region_gated_floor` consumption protocol is ambiguous and the two sides can silently diverge.
findings.location: What section / 'REUSE unchanged kernel primitives' bullet — 'score_code_novelty' description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the shared `verify.*` config coupling (both review domains governed by the same keys) in the plan, and note the implication that tuning affects both domains — or add a note that independent tuning will require per-domain keys in a future story.
findings.finding: The plan reuses `verify.novelty_drop_threshold`, `verify.novelty_priority_floor`, and `verify.novelty_drop_active` — keys previously scoped to plan-review — to also govern code-review behavior. This shared config coupling is introduced silently (the plan frames it as a feature: 'no NEW config key'), but it means tuning these keys for plan-review will directly affect code-review drop behavior and vice versa, with no interface or namespace boundary.
findings.location: ## What — Thresholds/activation (decided); AC item 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence under 'Thresholds/activation' such as: 'NOTE: these keys are now shared across plan-review and code-review — an operator tuning them affects both domains. Independent tuning is deferred to a follow-on story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned prompt-eval fixture or RED test (even a single golden example) that exercises the redefined `cites_prior_location` semantics — verifying the model distinguishes same-file/different-region from same-file/same-region — before the feature is considered validated.
findings.finding: The `cites_prior_location` field is redefined for code review (same file + ~10-line region) via prompt instruction alone. The plan explicitly flags this as 'critical' and calls out the risk of the model anchoring on the plan-review meaning, but no spike or probe is planned to validate that the LLM actually respects the redefined semantics reliably — this is an asserted behavioral assumption about instruction-following.
findings.location: ## What — 'cites_prior_location REDEFINED for code (critical)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one golden pair to the reviewer test fixtures: a prior finding at `foo.py:42` and a candidate at `foo.py:91` should yield `cites_prior_location=false`; a candidate at `foo.py:45` should yield `cites_prior_location=true`. Run these against the new `code_review_novelty` prompt as part of the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the use of `attest._hash_file` in `region_gate.py`, or expose a stable public helper (e.g. `attest.hash_file`) and reference that instead.
findings.finding: `region_gate.py` is specified to call `attest._hash_file` (a private/internal function from a different module) to hash the current file for region detection. This cross-component dependency on a private function is not acknowledged, justified, or protected by an interface boundary in the plan.
findings.location: ## What — NEW `region_gate.py`; 'hashes the current file (`attest._hash_file`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either rename `_hash_file` to `hash_file` in `attest` (making it a stable public interface) and update the plan to reference the public name, or add a one-line note in the plan justifying the private-function use and accepting the coupling risk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a latency target or time budget for the `region_gated_floor` novelty pass, and either confirm that serial per-finding LLM calls are acceptable at the expected finding-count ceiling or specify a batching/parallelism strategy to bound total latency and cost.
findings.finding: The `score_code_novelty` function issues one LLM sub-call per finding with no stated batching, parallelism, or latency target for the novelty-scoring pass as a whole, and the per-finding LLM call cost multiplier is unaddressed.
findings.location: What / 'Consume the reader' + 'NEW code-review novelty PROMPT' sections; AC line: 'rising_floor_drop, reshape_novelties, and decide.novelty are reused UNCHANGED (the thin score_code_novelty…)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or note bounding the expected finding count (e.g., 'at most N findings per review'), confirm serial calls are acceptable at that N, and optionally describe parallel dispatch (e.g., `asyncio.gather`) if latency is a concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to the FAIL-SAFE: at minimum, log the caught exception (with context: review key, finding id, error type) so that silent fallback is visible in logs/traces, and optionally increment a metric counter for monitoring.
findings.finding: The try/except fail-safe swallows exceptions silently — no logging, metric, or alert is specified for when the floor catches an error and falls back to the full unfiltered set, leaving operators with no visibility into degraded operation.
findings.location: What / FAIL-SAFE section: 'the whole floor is wrapped in try/except; any reader/hash/compare/novelty error returns the FULL unfiltered finding set (no drops)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the try/except block, add `logger.warning('region_gated_floor error, returning full set', exc_info=True, extra={'key': key})` before returning the unfiltered set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one end-to-end or dogfooding acceptance criterion that verifies the floor produces an observable, user-facing improvement (e.g., an integration test against a real prior code-review result showing a carryover finding is actually dropped, or a dogfooding note that a human reviewer confirmed reduced re-nag on a known-unchanged file).
findings.finding: The plan's validation mechanism for the value it delivers (stopping reviewer re-nag on unchanged code) is limited to mocked-unit tests; no end-to-end, dogfooding, or operational signal is specified to confirm the improvement is observable in real usage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (test bullet) / Why section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'End-to-end smoke test: given a recorded prior code-review result for a repo state and the same repo state re-reviewed, at least one previously-surfaced low-priority finding on an unchanged file is absent from the new verdict (dropped), confirming the floor is active in the real workflow path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test asserting that with `verify.novelty_drop_active = False`, `region_gated_floor` produces no drops even for unchanged-region, low-priority, low-novelty findings (covering the activation-gate code path in the new code-review integration).
findings.finding: The test plan omits an explicit test for the activation-gate path: when `verify.novelty_drop_active = False`, the floor must produce no drops even for unchanged-region low-priority findings. The flag is newly wired into code-review's floor (a new integration point), so its correct effect in THIS context should be verified — it cannot be assumed from plan-review's tests of the same key.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet (Tests section) / 'Thresholds/activation' paragraph in What
findings.suggested_fix: In the mocked-reader test suite, add a case: configure `novelty_drop_active=False`, present a finding that would otherwise drop (unchanged region, low priority, novelty below t_novel), and assert it is NOT dropped and carries no `drop_reason`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `output_schema="novelty"` (already auto-registered by the kernel at `verify.py:446`) can be used directly in the `score_code_novelty` `RunRequest`, making the new `code_review_novelty` registration in `contracts.register_contracts()` unnecessary; if a distinct name IS needed (e.g., for prompt-routing disambiguation), add a one-sentence justification to the plan.
findings.finding: The plan registers `code_review_novelty` as a NEW named contract (an alias for the identical `novelty_model` factory) when the kernel ALREADY auto-registers the same model under `"novelty"` (`verify.py:446: contracts.register_contract("novelty", novelty_model)`). A `RunRequest(output_schema="novelty")` would work without any new registration. Registering a second name for the same model factory is a config-surface-proliferation instance that cargo-cults the `plan_review_novelty` pattern without checking whether the existing `"novelty"` key already serves the need.
findings.location: What / NEW code-review novelty PROMPT + reviewer id section; AC item 2 (`code_review_novelty` contract registered)
findings.suggested_fix: Use `output_schema="novelty"` in the `RunRequest` and drop the `code_review_novelty` registration from `contracts.register_contracts()`. The kernel's auto-registration already covers this. If prompt routing requires a distinct key, document why the existing `"novelty"` key fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on: [revenued-thickset-dassie (c639)]` dependency to the plan and gate AC8/AC9 tests on the mocked reader interface being frozen (confirm c639's payload shape is final before starting).
findings.finding: The plan's primary reader dependency — `latest_code_review_result(key)` in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` — does not yet exist in the codebase (story c639 / revenued-thickset-dassie is in_progress, not committed). The plan's Scope section mentions this story as a prerequisite but does not enumerate it in a Dependencies field or guard the ACs to require its merge before implementation begins.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Depends on the reader/payload (revenued-thickset-dassie)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the plan's `region_gate.py` section, spell out the full import `from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest import _hash_file` and acknowledge this is a cross-sub-package private symbol reuse (or note whether a public wrapper should be extracted instead).
findings.finding: The plan references `attest._hash_file` as the hashing primitive for `region_gate.py` without specifying the full import path `rebar.llm.plan_review.attest._hash_file`. This is a private symbol (`_` prefix) in a different sub-package (`plan_review`), making the import non-obvious and a potential cross-subsystem coupling violation.
findings.location: NEW `region_gate.py` section: 'hashes the current file (`attest._hash_file`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'imported as `from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest import _hash_file, _hash_basis`' — the same reuse the c639 reader story already sanctions for `code_review/sidecar.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `reviewers/index.json` needs a `code-novelty` entry (explain: analogous to `plan-novelty`, which is absent from index.json, so index.json edit is OUT of scope).
findings.finding: The `code-novelty` reviewer id used in `RunRequest(reviewers=["code-novelty"])` is not discussed in relation to `reviewers/index.json`. The analogous `plan-novelty` reviewer is also absent from `index.json`, suggesting the index is not required for these pass sub-call reviewers — but the plan never explicitly states this, leaving the scope of the `index.json` edit ambiguous (in or out of scope).
findings.location: NEW code-review novelty PROMPT section + Scope list
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note: 'index.json: OUT of scope — `code-novelty` is a pass sub-call reviewer, not a full-featured gate reviewer; `plan-novelty` is similarly absent from index.json.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why `score_novelty` was not extended at the kernel level to also return `matched_prior_id` (e.g. a `score_novelty_with_ids` variant or an `include_ids` flag), and justify the consumer-layer `score_code_novelty` thin-wrapper approach over a kernel enhancement (T1(b) Rule-of-Three).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `score_code_novelty` as a new thin consumer-layer function parallel to the kernel's `score_novelty`, justified solely by the need to also return `matched_prior_id` for carryover labeling. The plan does not document why extending the kernel function itself (e.g. a `score_novelty_with_ids` returning `dict[int, tuple[float, str]]`, or adding an `include_ids: bool` flag to `score_novelty`) was considered and ruled out. Since plan-review's `_score_floor_novelty` is the second existing call site that ignores `matched_prior_id` (it doesn't use carryover labeling), both call sites could benefit from a single richer kernel function. Creating a parallel function at the consumer layer without this justification is a Rule-of-Three anti-repackaging concern under T1(b).
findings.location: What section — 'REUSE unchanged kernel primitives' bullet, and 'NEW `region_gated_floor`' / `score_code_novelty` description
findings.suggested_fix: Add a kernel-level `score_novelty_with_ids(findings, *, prior_findings, run_chunk, ...) -> dict[int, tuple[float, str]]` (or extend `score_novelty` to accept `include_ids=True`) in `review_kernel/verify.py`, returning `(novelty, matched_prior_id)` per index. Both plan-review's `_score_floor_novelty` (ignoring the id) and code-review's `score_code_novelty` (using it) become thin callers of the same kernel primitive, eliminating the parallel function entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit precondition assertion or import-guard in `region_gated_floor` that surfaces a clear error (rather than silently degrading) if `latest_code_review_result` is not importable, so the missing prerequisite is caught loudly during integration rather than silently swallowed by the fail-safe.
findings.finding: latest_code_review_result(key) is asserted to exist in the tree when this story is implemented, but that function is not yet present in the current codebase (search returns no hits in sidecar.py); the plan explicitly declares revenued-thickset-dassie as a prerequisite, but that ticket is still 'in_progress'. If this story is picked up before that prerequisite is merged and confirmed green, the region_gated_floor's reader call will fail at runtime with an ImportError.
findings.location: What section — 'Consume the reader' bullet; Scope — 'Depends on the reader/payload (revenued-thickset-dassie)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-of-module import check (or a lazy import inside the floor function that raises a descriptive RuntimeError rather than relying on the broad try/except) so a missing prerequisite produces a visible error in tests, not a silent no-op.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `cites_prior_location` redefinition at the schema level: either add a code-review-specific description override when registering `code_review_novelty` (e.g. via a wrapper factory that overrides the field description), or document explicitly in the AC that prompt-only redefinition is sufficient and why (with reference to the pink-elephant risk mitigation in the prompt design).
findings.finding: The `cites_prior_location` field in the shared `novelty_model` (registered as `code_review_novelty`) carries the description and default semantics of a plan-review concept ('same plan location/section/AC line') at the Pydantic model level. The plan's redefinition ('same source file AND approximately same line region') lives only in the prompt text of `code_review_novelty.md`. If the prompt is ever bypassed, truncated, or the model anchors on the schema field description (a known pink-elephant risk with shared schemas), the redefinition is invisible. The plan's AC says to 'firmly redefine it' but the firm definition has no schema-level co-location.
findings.location: What section / 'cites_prior_location REDEFINED for code (critical)' bullet; AC item 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'novel' with 'low-novelty (carryover)' in AC4 and the AC9 first test case to match `rising_floor_drop`'s actual semantics (drops findings whose novelty score falls below t_novel, i.e., carryovers, not genuinely new findings).
findings.finding: AC4 and the first test scenario (AC9) both use the word 'novel' to describe the condition under which a finding is DROPPED, but `rising_floor_drop` drops findings with LOW novelty scores (carryovers/re-nags), not findings with HIGH novelty. An executor following the AC literally ('drops a finding ONLY when novel AND low-priority') could implement the predicate in reverse — dropping genuinely new findings — which contradicts the system's stated purpose of suppressing re-nags.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('drops a finding ONLY when novel AND low-priority AND REGION_UNCHANGED') and AC9 first test case ('unchanged-region low-priority novel → dropped')
findings.suggested_fix: AC4: 'region_gated_floor drops a finding ONLY when it is LOW-NOVELTY (carryover, novelty < t_novel) AND low-priority (priority < floor) AND REGION_UNCHANGED; REGION_CHANGED and REGION_UNKNOWN always raise; dropped findings carry drop_reason = "novelty-region".' AC9 first test: 'unchanged-region low-priority low-novelty (carryover) → dropped'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `rising_floor_drop` drops HIGH-novelty (novel, `novelty > t_novel`) or LOW-novelty (carryover, `novelty < t_novel`) findings, and confirm this is consistent with the existing plan-review implementation, the AC item 4 drop condition, and the carryover-labeling behavior (low-novelty = kept+labeled).
findings.finding: The plan states `rising_floor_drop` is reused UNCHANGED, but the drop condition as specified in AC item 4 ('drops a finding ONLY when novel AND low-priority AND REGION_UNCHANGED') drops HIGH-novelty findings on unchanged code — the opposite of a classic rising-floor that drops LOW-novelty (carryover) findings. If `rising_floor_drop` drops low-novelty items in plan-review (carryover suppression), it cannot also drop high-novelty items in code-review when 'reused UNCHANGED'. An implementer reading both the What section ('reuse… verbatim') and AC item 4 ('when novel… dropped') would be sent in two directions.
findings.location: What section — 'rising_floor_drop… verbatim'; AC item 4 — 'drops a finding ONLY when novel AND low-priority AND REGION_UNCHANGED'
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is to drop novel (high-novelty) findings on unchanged code, AC item 4 is correct but the 'verbatim reuse' claim needs a note that code-review and plan-review use the SAME predicate because it already drops high-novelty low-priority findings (confirm this matches plan-review's actual behavior). If plan-review drops LOW-novelty findings, code-review needs a different predicate or the AC item 4 condition must be corrected to 'NOT novel (carryover) AND low-priority AND REGION_UNCHANGED'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned ADR (or CLAUDE.md update) capturing the region-gate architectural decision and why it deliberately diverges from plan-review's whole-artifact floor.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate architectural divergence from plan-review's whole-artifact floor (a per-citation region gate) — explicitly called out as 'the deliberate divergence' — but no ADR update or new ADR is planned despite the project having an active ADR system (ADR 0008 is cited). This novel pipeline-stage design decision will be invisible to future maintainers.
findings.location: ## What — region_gated_floor description; ## Scope; AC list
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR entry (e.g. ADR-00NN: Code-review region-gated floor) to ## Scope covering: (a) the per-citation region gate rationale, (b) the deliberate config-key reuse and its shared-tuning implication, (c) the UNKNOWN=raise safety invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of `verdict.coaching` in the produce path and confirm (in the plan or an AC) that the strip is the sole mutation and occurs before any other consumer reads it, OR produce a new coaching list rather than mutating in place.
findings.finding: The plan mutates the verdict's `coaching` list (stripping notes whose `finding_refs` point at `carried_from`-stamped findings) without enumerating all consumers of that list or confirming the mutation is safe if the coaching list is read elsewhere before or after the strip.
findings.location: What / Carryover labeling section: 'the coach already ran in-workflow, so the floor strips coaching notes whose `finding_refs` point at a `carried_from`-stamped finding from the verdict's `coaching` list'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace in-place list mutation with a filtered copy: `verdict['coaching'] = [n for n in verdict.get('coaching', []) if n['finding_ref'] not in carried_ids]`, and document in a comment that this is the only coaching mutation in the produce path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan whether the Gerrit voter path goes through `_run_code_review_gate` (and thus the floor) or bypasses it; if it bypasses, either scope voter.py in or document the deliberate exclusion.
findings.finding: The Gerrit voter path (`voter.py`) is an unenumerated consumer of `sidecar.emit`. The plan's `region_gated_floor` is applied in `_run_code_review_gate` (the produce-path), but if the Gerrit voter calls `sidecar.emit` independently (bypassing `_run_code_review_gate`), the floor would not apply to Gerrit-triggered reviews.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py (invoke the floor before emit)'; c639 plan: 'voter.py::emit_code_review_artifact → sidecar.emit'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
